ground_truth.title: 5 Trends from Nutrition 2025
ground_truth.author: Dylan Bailey, MS, RD, FAND
ground_truth.main_content:
The Nutrition 2025 meeting hosted annually by the American Society for Nutrition (ASN) brought together leading experts, thought leaders, and clinicians to Orlando to showcase the latest in nutrition science and practice. I've compiled 5 trends that stood out to me while walking the expo hall and sitting in sessions over 4 days, with relevant takeaways for nutrition communications professionals.

1. From Lab to Literature, Artificial Intelligence Has Strong Showing
Artificial intelligence (AI) made a strong showing at Nutrition 2025 as researchers demonstrated its growing role in nutrition science – from lab to literature. AI is reshaping research and publishing, with experts from academia and industry sharing how they use AI tools to accelerate and improve data analysis and literature reviews (this was also prevalent in the poster sessions as well). Beyond scientific publishing, AI is also driving discovery of novel nutrients and bioactive compounds.

2. Science StorEATelling
This year's conference underscored the value of culinary experiences in bringing nutrition science to life. Rather than "dry" lectures alone, some lunchtime programs invited attendees to immerse themselves in the science through food – literally and figuratively consuming the science. Such interactive learning experiences allowed participants not just to hear about nutrition evidence, but to taste and see it in action.

3. GLP-1 Weight-Loss Drugs Steal the Spotlight With Nutrition Alongside
It's no surprise that GLP-1 agonist drugs (like semaglutide and tirzepatide) were everywhere at Nutrition 2025 – these anti-obesity medications are transforming weight management and were a hot topic in hallways and sessions alike. Researchers presented cutting-edge findings on how these drugs "modulate appetite-regulating circuits and metabolic pathways," offering unprecedented weight loss outcomes. But a clear theme was that medication alone isn't a magic bullet. Across multiple talks, experts emphasized the need for comprehensive strategies combining pharmacology with lifestyle changes including nutrition interventions or hydration needs (see here and here, #client).

4. The Ultra-Processed Foods Debate Isn't Going Anywhere
Sessions on ultra-processed foods (UPFs) were abundant at Nutrition 2025. A dedicated session on UPFs was widely attended, where researchers presented new data from the RESTRUCTURE trial, which is probing how the sensory properties of foods (like texture and eating speed) influence calorie intake and satiety. Consensus at the conference was that UPFs will remain a central research and communication topic.

5. Protein Science Continues to Dominate
If one nutrient reigned supreme at Nutrition 2025, it was protein. From muscle health to metabolic advantages, protein was at the center of many presentations, reflecting its ongoing star status in nutrition science. A full-day special "Dietary Protein for Global Human Health" workshop brought together leading experts to share the latest research on protein quality and requirements across populations. A key focus was on protein quality – not just quantity – and how better metrics like DIAAS (Digestible Indispensable Amino Acid Score) can inform global nutrition strategies.

Experts discussed using high-quality proteins to combat malnutrition and other health issues in vulnerable groups (e.g. children, the elderly) and even presented ideas for integrating protein quality considerations into dietary guidelines. Beyond this, numerous studies and sessions presented at the conference examined protein's role in satiety and weight management – reinforcing findings that higher-protein diets can aid in appetite control and body composition.

Takeaway for Communicators: Leverage the public's interest in protein – but do so responsibly. The continued spotlight on protein means audiences are receptive to messages about getting enough protein and choosing high-quality sources. Communicators should translate the latest science (e.g. explain what "protein quality" means and why not all proteins are equal) into consumer-friendly advice.

Artificial intelligence (AI) made a strong showing at Nutrition 2025 as researchers demonstrated its growing role in nutrition science
But a clear theme was that medication alone isn’t a magic bullet.
Sessions on ultra-processed foods (UPFs) were abundant at Nutrition 2025.
If one nutrient reigned supreme at Nutrition 2025, it was protein.
